course_description: This foundational course introduces students to evidence-based therapeutic approaches and the core psychological skills needed to apply them. It covers fundamental concepts of intake and risk assessment, case planning and treatment, and covers Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (CBT), Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT), trauma-informed care, and Motivational Interviewing. Students will develop key therapeutic skills for case formulation, risk assessment, and intervention planning while integrating research evidence into psychological practice. Emphasis in working across diverse populations, including Indigenous peoples, and considering the social determinants of mental health. Through theoretical learning, case studies, observation, modelling, role-playing, and practical applications, students will gain competencies and a strong foundation in evidence-based psychological practices.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
It is a condition of courses in accredited postgraduate psychology programs that students are required to attend coursework lectures, tutorials, and workshops. As such, Course Coordinators are required to document class attendance.
If you cannot attend, it is expected that you inform the Course Coordinator as soon as possible and provide any relevant documentation to justify your absence. Students who have more than two unexplained and approved absences from the workshops and tutorials may be asked to meet with the Program Director to explain why their attendance is not meeting the expected standard.
Satisfactory coursework attendance is required in order for the Program Director to make a recommendation to the PsyBA in respect to student application for general registration. Thus, repeated failure to attend class without appropriate notification and documentation may result in students having to complete an additional period of supervised practice or further coursework in order to satisfy the requirements of the program.
course_aims:
This course aims to provide students with the foundational professional skills relevant to practicing psychology. Specific aims are to:

a) develop skills in the core techniques of effective psychological intervention

b) develop the capacity to perform appropriate case formulations in association with individualised treatment plans based on evidence-based principles

c) become competent in accurately assessing suicide risk and intervening appropriately

d) self-appraise the effectiveness of their use of therapeutic strategies and self-direct enhancement of their therapeutic work

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the foundations of evidence-based therapy and demonstrate knowledge of core principles in psychological interventions, that include face to face and digital applications
Develop therapeutic skills and apply foundational therapeutic strategies in practice
Perform psychological risk assessments: assess and manage suicide risk and other psychological risks effectively.
Develop structured case formulations and treatment plans using evidence-based principles
Engage in self-reflection for professional practice and self-care
Apply culturally responsive practices in their work with clients from culturally and linguistically diverse and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander backgrounds.

assessment_details.title: Intake interview and self-reflection
assessment_details.task_description: Students will conduct and record a 15-20 minute role-play intake interview with a peer acting as the client, demonstrating competency in foundational microskills and intake procedures including establishing rapport, discussing confidentiality, exploring presenting concerns, and summarising key information. Following the interview, students will complete a self-evaluation using the Interview Checklist (available on learn.uq) and submit a brief written reflection identifying strengths, areas for development, and aspects they found challenging. A one-page dot-point summary of peer feedback received must also be included. This assessment develops practical interviewing skills alongside capacity for self-reflection and integration of feedback. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission is via Blackboard. If required, there are videoing facilities within the UQ Psychology Clinic consult rooms.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
For full details please review the School of Psychology Student Guidelines - Extensions and Deferred quiz/exam.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Risk Assessment Competency
assessment_details.due_date: 13/03/2026 Individual times will be allocated on this day via learn.uq
assessment_details.task_description: This is a competency-based assessment for conducting a suicide and safety risk assessment role-play with a simulated client presenting with suicide risk factors. The assessment evaluates the student's ability to explore suicidal ideation, identify protective and risk factors, determine level of risk, and collaboratively develop an appropriate safety plan. Following the role-play, students will complete a written formulation, plan, and a reflection on performance. Individual timeslots (40 minutes) will be allocated to each student. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
For full details please review the School of Psychology Student Guidelines - Extensions and Deferred quiz/exam.
If you require a deferred competency, please submit your appropriate documentation (as per the extension policy) to the course coordinator to arrange for an alternate time to complete your competency. If you are not able to make it to the competency assessment on the day, please contact the course-coordinator directly for planning purposes.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Skill Demonstration and Clinical Reasoning Competency
assessment_details.category: Practical/ Demonstration, Presentation, Reflection, Role play/ Simulation
assessment_details.due_date: 15/05/2026 18/05/2026 5:00 pm The role-play will occur on the 15th of May and individual time-slots will be allocated. The written component will be due on 18th May.
assessment_details.task_description: This is a two-part competency-based assessment. Part 1: Students will be presented with a case study and required to demonstrate an appropriate CBT skill (allocated in role-play) with a simulated client. Following the role-play, students will be asked to verbally reflect on their performance. Part 2: Following the in-person role-play and reflections, students will be able to take home their notes and are required to write a case-note, as one might to do in practice, including a session note, a formulation, and a treatment plan moving forward. The students will then be asked a series of questions for student's to demonstrate their clinical reasoning (provided ahead of time via learn.uq). This is required to be submitted via Turn-it-in 48 hours after completion of their role-play. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.due_date: 23/02/2026 - 25/05/2026 The worksheet and reflections are to be submitted one week after the relevant workshop via the Assessment Page on learn.uq. The final self-reflection piece is due on 29th May.
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to complete a worksheet that outlines a therapy skill learnt in class, and asked to provide a reflection on the therapy skills completed on the worksheet and practiced in class. The worksheet and reflections will be submitted the following week via Assessment Page on learn.uq. The final self-reflection piece is worth 15% and requires students to reflect on their therapies skill with regard to professional competencies for psychologists, with a particular focus on digital health, culturally responsive practices in their work with clients from culturally and linguistically diverse and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander backgrounds, and trauma-informed care. learning_activities.topic: Workshop 1 Clinical Skills Intensive: including an overview of the course, microskills, intake interview, core therapeutic processes
Workshop 2 Clinical skills continued, Risk Assessment & Crisis Management.
Practical 1 Skills practice in conducting a suicide risk assessment
Workshop 3 Introduction to CBT: Core CBT principles, therapeutic relationship, CBT formulation, session structure
Practical 2 Skills practice in applying the foundations of CBT
Workshop 4 Behavioural Interventions in CBT Part 1: Behavioural activation, activity scheduling and monitoring, Action Plans
Workshop 5 Behavioural Interventions in CBT Part 2: Behavioural techniques for anxiety, relaxation, exposure principles, graded exposure, habituation
Practical 3 Skills practice in applying behavioural interventions (e.g., behavioural activation, activity monitoring, graded exposure)
Workshop 6 Cognitive Interventions in CBT Part 1:
Practical 4 Skills practice in cognitive interventions: Identifying thoughts and thought records
Workshop 7 Cognitive Interventions in CBT Part 2: Responding to automatic thoughts, addressing perpetuating factors (safety behaviours, cognitive avoidance), worry management, Beliefs and Schemas
Practical 5 Skills practice in cognitive interventions: Cognitive restructuring, Behavioural Experiments, addressing schemas

Workshop 8 Mindfulness and ACT Part 1: Integrating mindfulness into CBT; Conceptual Foundations of Acceptance Commitment Therapy, The "Open Up" processes (acceptance, defusion, present moment awareness)
Practical 6 Skills practice in mindfulness and acceptance exercises
Workshop 9 ACT Part 2: The "Engage in Life" processes, Committed Action and Goals, practical application of ACT
Practical 7 Skills Practice in defusion and committed action
Workshop 10 Motivational Interviewing Part 1: Spirit of MI, OARS skills, change talk
Practical 8 Skills Practice in MI: Reflective listening, open questions
Workshop 11 Motivational Interviewing Part 2: Eliciting change talk, developing discrepancy, rolling with resistance
Practical 9 Skills practice in MI: Complex reflections, eliciting change talk
Workshop 12 Transcultural formulations
Workshop 13 Indigenous Social and Emotional Wellbeing: Cultural safety, SEWB framework, yarning methodology

Workshop 14 Digital Tools in Practice: Digital Mental health overview, evaluating and selecting digital tools, telehealth competencies, and practical integration and emerging frontiers in digital health.
Workshop 15 Working in Groups and Group Processes: Foundations of group therapy, Group development and dynamics, facilitator skills and practical applications.
